title: Medication That Cures Too Well
logline: A biotech scientist announces a breakthrough drug that cures a chronic disease—then dies days later in a staged ‘accident’ as her company abruptly withdraws the product from the market.

setting.primary_location: Biotech campus and nearby hospital/commuter corridor
setting.timeframe: One week spanning the drug announcement, a fatal incident, and the sudden market withdrawal

premise.victim_profile: Lead scientist and co-inventor of the therapy
premise.inciting_event: Company issues an urgent withdrawal and nondisclosure memo within 72 hours of the death

suspects.name: The CEO
suspects.role: Company head balancing IPO pressure and catastrophic liability

suspects.name: The Head of Regulatory
suspects.role: Gatekeeper of filings; knows what was (and wasn’t) disclosed

suspects.name: The Lead Investor
suspects.role: Board powerbroker with motives tied to market timing and competitors

suspects.name: The Research Rival
suspects.role: Internal competitor whose program was sidelined by the breakthrough

digital_evidence_targets: Lab notebook scans and experiment logs
Access/badge logs and security incident report
Public-facing press release and retraction statements
Social posts from patient advocacy groups

spatial_tool.concept: Floor plan of the lab wing + parking/garage access points to reason about tampering and movement

trojan_horse.concept: A routine ‘safety recall’ bulletin that looks like bureaucratic noise but quietly proves the ‘accident’ was engineered.
trojan_horse.purpose: Turns a mundane corporate document into the key that collapses the staged-accident narrative

tone.style: Clinical realism; regulatory pressure; ethical dread; whistleblower tension
